1.lecture:
§1 · The six-family taxonomy

Processing operations transform a workpart. Shaping covers four distinct routes: casting and molding (start liquid), deformation — rolling, forging, extrusion, drawing, sheet forming (start solid, move it), particulate processing (press and sinter powders), and material removal (machining, grinding, cutting away). Property-enhancing operations — heat treatment above all — leave geometry unchanged and rebuild microstructure instead. Surface processing — cleaning, coating, plating — changes only the surface. Assembly operations join parts: permanent joining (welding, brazing, adhesives) and mechanical assembly (bolting, riveting, press fits). A seventh box, material handling and inspection, adds no value directly but consumes most of the lead time in a real plant.

§2 · Primary vs. secondary, and reading a real process chain

Primary processing creates the basic shape — a billet, a casting — from raw material. Secondary processing refines that shape further — machining a bearing seat, grinding a journal onto an already-cast or already-forged part. The working method: trace any product from raw material to dispatch, naming which family each step belongs to. A rebar's chain is casting (shaping, primary) then hot rolling (shaping, deformation) then cooling, cutting, and bundling (handling) — two value-adding families for one product. A power-cable conductor's chain runs longer: wire drawing (deformation), annealing (property-enhancing), stranding (mechanical assembly), insulation extrusion over the conductor (shaping, polymer), spooling and test (handling, inspection) — four families in a single line.

§3 · Why this taxonomy matters more than it looks

By tonnage, the world's metal is shaped overwhelmingly by casting and deformation, not machining — machining refines a surface or a fit onto a shape one of those other families already created. An engineer walking into "manufacturing" expecting mainly a machine shop is statistically far more likely to meet a caster, a rolling mill, a press, or a filling line as the dominant floor space. Casting and deformation are not legacy technology on the way out; they remain the primary-shaping backbone of everything heavier than electronics. What has changed around them is instrumentation, automation, and control — exactly where this curriculum's later Industry 4.0 material points.

§4 · Working method — reading any plant with one map

Given an unfamiliar plant or process line: (1) identify the raw material entering; (2) trace each transformation step and classify it into one of the six families; (3) note where primary shaping ends and secondary refinement begins; (4) recognize that handling and inspection, though value-neutral, often dominate real lead time and hide a large share of any real improvement opportunity. This single procedure reads a steel mill, a cable plant, or a food-filling hall with equal clarity.

Key fact

By tonnage, casting and deformation shape the large majority of the world's metal — machining refines a shape another process already created, it does not usually create the shape itself.

quiz.q:
Classifying a bracket production chain

Problem. A steel bracket is produced: (a) cut from a continuously cast billet, (b) hot-forged to rough shape, (c) heat-treated, (d) machined for mounting holes, (e) painted. Name the family for each step.
quiz.solution: Solution. (a) shaping/casting, primary. (b) shaping/deformation. (c) property-enhancing. (d) shaping/material removal, secondary. (e) surface processing.

quiz.q:
Classifying a conductor process chain

Problem. A cable conductor’s chain is: wire drawing → annealing → stranding → polymer extrusion over the conductor → spooling. Identify which steps are primary shaping, which are property-modifying, and which are assembly.
quiz.solution: Solution. Wire drawing: primary shaping (deformation). Annealing: property-enhancing, not shaping at all — it restores ductility lost to the drawing passes. Stranding: mechanical assembly (twisting wires together). Polymer extrusion: shaping (a second, different material, primary for the insulation layer). Spooling: handling.

quiz.q:
Why "machining" is the wrong first guess

Problem. Using tonnage reasoning, explain why a new mechanical engineer expecting to mainly encounter machining on a first plant tour is likely mistaken, and name the two families more likely to dominate by volume.
quiz.solution: Solution. By tonnage, casting and deformation dominate world metal production; machining is a secondary process applied after a primary shape from one of those two families already exists. A plant tour is statistically far more likely to feature a caster, a rolling mill, or a press occupying the dominant floor space than a machine shop.

quiz.q: Casting and molding are classified as:
choices: A shaping operation that starts liquid
quiz.solution: Casting starts from a liquid and freezes to the cavity's shape.

quiz.q: Deformation processes (rolling, forging, extrusion, drawing) are classified as:
choices: Shaping operations that start solid and move material
quiz.solution: These all move already-solid material into a new shape.

quiz.q: Heat treatment is classified as:
choices: A property-enhancing operation — geometry unchanged, microstructure rebuilt
quiz.solution: Heat treatment changes internal structure, not external geometry.

quiz.q: Welding and brazing are classified as:
quiz.solution: They join separate parts into one, permanently.

quiz.q: Material handling and inspection:
choices: Add no value directly but often dominate real lead time
Are the highest value-adding operations in a plant
Never appear in a real process chain
quiz.solution: Necessary, but value-neutral — and usually where most elapsed time actually goes.

quiz.q: Primary processing:
choices: Creates the basic shape from raw material
Refines a shape another process already created
quiz.solution: Primary shaping is the first shape-creating step from raw material.

quiz.q: Secondary processing:
choices: Refines a shape a primary process already created
quiz.solution: Machining a bearing seat onto an already-cast part is the classic example.

quiz.q: By tonnage, the world's metal is shaped mostly by:
quiz.solution: Machining refines a shape; it rarely creates the bulk shape itself.

1.kuwait:
A rebar's process chain in a regional steel mill runs scrap through melting, then continuous casting (shaping: casting, primary), then hot rolling (shaping: deformation), then cooling, cutting, and bundling (handling) — two value-adding families producing one product. A power-cable conductor at a regional cable plant runs a longer chain: rod drawing (deformation), annealing (property-enhancing), stranding (mechanical assembly), insulation extrusion over the conductor (shaping: polymer), spooling and test (handling, inspection) — four families in one line, which is why cable-plant engineers tend to be generalists rather than specialists in a single family.

Reading an unfamiliar plant with this six-family map, rather than assuming every operation is either "the machine shop" or "the assembly line," is often the fastest way a new engineer orients on an unfamiliar floor — a dairy filling hall, a fabrication yard, and a steel mill all decompose into the same six boxes, just in very different proportions.

2.lecture:
§1 · Casting is a controlled thermal collapse

A casting process melts metal, pours it into a cavity shaped like the part, and lets it solidify — the oldest manufacturing process still run at industrial scale, surviving because nothing else produces complex internal geometry in one operation this cheaply. Expendable-mold processes (sand, shell, investment) destroy the mold to retrieve the part — cheap tooling, high geometric freedom. Permanent-mold processes (die casting, continuous casting) reuse a metal mold — expensive tooling that demands volume to pay for itself. This lecture develops the physics common to all of them using sand casting, since its assumptions are cleanest: pay energy to superheat the melt, spend that superheat transporting metal through the gating system before it freezes in the channels, then extract all of it back out through the mold wall at a rate the mold controls — and the volume deficit left behind by that extraction is the riser's problem.

§2 · Gating: Bernoulli with consequences

The gating system has one job: deliver the melt to the cavity before superheat runs out, without turbulence tearing air and oxide into the stream. Applying Bernoulli's equation from the free metal surface in the pouring basin to the sprue base — both stations near atmospheric pressure, the basin kept topped up so its surface barely moves — gives Torricelli's result, \( v=\sqrt{2gh} \), with \(h\) the vertical head from the free metal surface, never from the sprue's own top. Because velocity rises with depth, a parallel-walled sprue lets the falling stream neck away from its walls and aspirate air; matching the sprue's taper to the falling jet via continuity, \( A_t/A_b=\sqrt{h_b/h_t} \), removes that defect with one line of algebra. The flow is, in practice, always fully turbulent (Reynolds numbers in the tens of thousands for liquid metal) — the designer's real tools are geometry (taper, gate area, filters), not pouring speed.

§3 · Solidification, and the equation that runs the foundry

A pure metal freezes at one temperature; an alloy freezes across a range, liquidus to solidus, with a mushy zone of dendritic growth between them. For a sand mold — a poor enough conductor that the mold, not the metal, controls the rate — treating the mold wall as a semi-infinite solid gives a clean result: total solidification time follows Chvorinov's rule, \[ TST=C_m\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{n},\qquad n\approx2\text{ for sand molds}, \] where \(V/A\) is the casting's modulus. \(C_m\) is measured once from a test casting in the same sand and alloy, then the rule ranks every shape: thick, chunky regions (high modulus) freeze last; thin fins (low modulus) freeze first. This mold-controlled assumption fails in a water-cooled metal die, where interface and metal-side resistance dominate instead and \(n\) drifts toward 1 — carrying \(n=2\) into a die-casting estimate is a real, not cosmetic, error.

§4 · Shrinkage, and the riser that pays for it

Metal contracts three separate times, compensated by three different people. Liquid contraction (pouring temperature down to freezing) is cheap — the gating system simply supplies a little more liquid while everything is still fluid. Solidification shrinkage — the liquid-to-solid density jump, roughly \(6\)–\(7\)% for aluminum, \(2.5\)–\(3\)% for low-carbon steel — is the riser's job: a sacrificial reservoir that must still be liquid, and must stay liquid longer than everything it feeds, or the shrinkage cavity forms in the part instead. Chvorinov turns "stay liquid longer" into arithmetic: sizing the riser so \( TST_{riser}=1.25\times TST_{casting} \) — a 25% margin covering what the model ignores — translates directly into a required riser modulus, \( (V/A)_{riser}=\sqrt{1.25}\,(V/A)_{casting} \). Solid contraction (freezing point down to room temperature) has no liquid left to feed it at all; the patternmaker builds the pattern oversize by a shrinkage allowance instead, so the part contracts down onto the drawing.

Key rule — Chvorinov (1940)

\[ TST = C_m\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{n},\quad n\approx2\ (\text{sand}).\qquad (V/A)_{riser}=\sqrt{1.25}\,(V/A)_{casting}. \]

quiz.q:
Sprue base velocity and taper

Problem. A pouring basin holds 50 mm of metal above a 200 mm sprue. Find the velocity at the sprue base, and the top-to-base area ratio needed to prevent aspiration.
quiz.solution: Solution. Head at the base: \(h_b=0.050+0.200=0.250\ \text{m}\). \( v=\sqrt{2\times9.81\times0.250}\approx2.21\ \text{m/s} \). Head at the top is the basin depth alone, \(h_t=0.050\ \text{m}\): \( A_t/A_b=\sqrt{h_b/h_t}=\sqrt{0.250/0.050}=\sqrt5\approx2.24 \) — the sprue narrows by that factor from top to base.

quiz.q:
Sizing a riser by Chvorinov’s rule

Problem. A steel plate casting has modulus \((V/A)_c=2.31\ \text{cm}\), and the mold constant is \(C_m=2.0\ \text{min/cm}^2\). Find the casting’s solidification time and the modulus a riser needs to satisfy the 25% rule.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( TST_c=2.0\times2.31^2\approx10.7\ \text{min} \). Required \( TST_r=1.25\times10.7\approx13.3\ \text{min} \), so \( (V/A)_r=\sqrt{13.3/2.0}\approx2.58\ \text{cm} \) — larger than the casting’s modulus, confirming the riser freezes last.

quiz.q:
Cube vs. sphere — same volume, different modulus

Problem. A cube and a sphere of the same alloy, same volume 512 cm\(^3\) (the cube is 8 cm on a side), are poured in the same sand, \(C_m=1.8\ \text{min/cm}^2\). Which solidifies last, and by roughly how much?
quiz.solution: Solution. Cube: \(A=6\times8^2=384\ \text{cm}^2\), \(V/A=1.33\ \text{cm}\), \(TST=1.8\times1.33^2\approx3.2\ \text{min}\). Sphere: \(r\approx4.96\ \text{cm}\), \(A=4\pi r^2\approx309\ \text{cm}^2\), \(V/A=r/3\approx1.65\ \text{cm}\), \(TST\approx1.8\times1.65^2\approx4.9\ \text{min}\). The sphere solidifies last, by roughly 1.7 minutes — over 50% longer — despite identical volume, because Chvorinov ranks by surface area exposed, not volume.

quiz.q: The velocity at the base of a sprue is given by:
choices: v = √(2gh), with h measured from the free metal surface
v = √(2gh), with h the sprue's own length
v is independent of head height
quiz.solution: The datum is the free metal surface in the basin, not the sprue top — a classic source of error.

quiz.q: A tapered sprue is used mainly to:
choices: Prevent the falling stream from pulling away from the walls and aspirating air
quiz.solution: Matching the taper to the accelerating jet keeps the stream sealed against the sprue wall.

quiz.q: Chvorinov's rule ranks casting sections by:
quiz.solution: A shape hiding more volume behind less surface freezes later, regardless of total size.

quiz.q: Chvorinov's exponent n≈2 specifically assumes:
choices: Mold-controlled heat flow, as in an insulating sand mold
quiz.solution: In a metal die, interface/metal-side resistance dominates instead and n drifts toward 1.

quiz.q: Liquid contraction, the first of the three contractions, is compensated by:
choices: The gating system supplying a little extra liquid while still fluid
quiz.solution: Cheap and automatic, as long as the gating system stays connected and fluid.

quiz.q: Solidification shrinkage — the liquid-to-solid density jump — is compensated by:
quiz.solution: This is specifically what a riser exists to feed.

quiz.q: Solid contraction, after the part is fully frozen, is compensated by:
choices: The patternmaker building the pattern oversize (shrinkage allowance)
It cannot be compensated at all
quiz.solution: No liquid remains to feed this contraction — only an oversized pattern corrects for it.

quiz.q: A riser sized by the 25% rule has a required modulus:
choices: Larger than the casting's own modulus, by a factor of √1.25
Smaller than the casting's own modulus
Exactly equal to the casting's modulus
quiz.solution: A larger modulus means a longer solidification time — the riser must outlast the casting.

2.kuwait:
A foundry engineer designing a gating system runs §2 exactly as derived: head measured from the free metal surface, sprue taper set by the area-ratio rule, and gate sizing chosen to slow the stream below the velocity that entrains oxide — get the datum for h wrong, and every downstream number (velocity, fill time, taper) inherits the error.

The same Chvorinov analysis that sizes a riser also scales up directly to continuous casting — a solid shell growing inward from a water-cooled mold at steady state, governed by the identical square-root-of-time physics. A regional steel mill running a billet caster around the clock is this lesson's physics in continuous operation rather than a single pour; that machine gets its own full treatment next lesson.

3.lecture:
§1 · Patterns, allowances, and molding sand

A mold cavity is deliberately wrong, in four specific ways. The shrinkage (patternmaker's) allowance builds the pattern oversize so solid contraction shrinks the casting back onto the drawing — it cannot compensate solidification shrinkage, which is the riser's job (Lesson 2). Draft, a taper of roughly \(1\)–\(3^\circ\) on surfaces perpendicular to the parting line, lets the pattern withdraw without tearing the sand. Machining allowance leaves extra stock on surfaces to be machined. Distortion allowance pre-bends a pattern opposite to its predicted distortion. Internal geometry comes from cores — bonded-sand shapes that are also a gas source and a restraint on contraction. Green sand itself is specified by test numbers, not by feel: green compressive strength (too low risks collapse; too high traps gas and resists contraction), permeability (how well gases vent — low permeability plus wet sand is a blowhole problem waiting to happen), and moisture (too dry crumbles, too wet boils).

§2 · Fluidity, misruns, and cold shuts

Fluidity, in the foundry sense, is measured, not derived — the distance a standard test channel fills before the stream freezes shut. It is a race between the advancing stream and mold-controlled heat loss, and it rises with superheat, falls with a wide freezing range (a mushy alloy clogs the channel before it is fully solid), and falls with a stiff surface oxide film. When the race is lost, the resulting defect has two names with two different causes: a misrun is a section the metal never reached at all; a cold shut is where two streams met but were each too cold to fuse, leaving an oxide-lined seam that can look deceptively like a crack. The fixes differ — a misrun asks for more superheat or faster filling; a cold shut additionally asks for gating that avoids opposed, long-path streams.

§3 · Gas porosity, and Sievert's law derived

Liquid metal dissolves gas far more readily than solid metal does — that solubility cliff at the freezing front is the entire mechanism of gas porosity. Hydrogen dissolves by splitting into two atoms, \( \text{H}_2\rightleftharpoons2[\text{H}] \), so the equilibrium constant carries the dissolved concentration squared against the gas partial pressure: \( K(T)=[\text{H}]^2/p_{\text{H}_2} \). Solving gives Sievert's law, \[ [\text{H}]=S_0(T)\sqrt{p/p_0}, \] a square root that is not an empirical fit — it is the stoichiometry of the dissolution reaction doing algebra. \(S_0\) rises steeply with temperature, the hidden price of superheat: a hotter melt runs farther (§02) and dissolves more hydrogen from every damp tool or humid hour. At solidification, solubility drops sharply, the rejected gas recombines, and the result is pinholes — small, spherical, smooth-walled pores, their roundness the signature of having grown as bubbles in liquid. Mold and core gas — steam and binder combustion products that could not vent through the sand — is the other source, producing larger, often subsurface blowholes instead.

§4 · Shrinkage porosity, and the working method for a defect

Solidification shrinkage the riser fails to feed becomes a cavity, at two scales. Macro-shrinkage is an open pipe or closed cavity at an unfed hot spot — a feeding-path failure, visible once sectioned. Microporosity is subtler: in the mushy zone of a wide-freezing-range alloy, the last interdendritic liquid is cut off by the surrounding dendrite forest and shrinks in place, leaving jagged, angular, tree-lined voids — a morphology that contrasts directly with gas porosity's round, smooth pores. The working method for any defect: (1) read the morphology — round and smooth points to gas, jagged and angular points to shrinkage; (2) trace it to its physical cause using the taxonomy — solubility drop, volume deficit, lost heat race, restrained contraction, mold failure, or melt cleanliness; (3) let the cause pick the fix, not the appearance alone, since a misrun and a cold shut can look similar but need different corrections.

Key tool — Sievert's law

\[ [\text{H}] = S_0(T)\sqrt{p/p_0} \]

A square-root relation from reaction stoichiometry — the mechanism behind pinhole porosity.

quiz.q:
Reading a defect’s morphology

Problem. A sectioned casting shows small, round, smooth-walled pores scattered near the upper surface. A second casting shows jagged, angular voids threaded between visible dendrite arms near its thermal center. Identify each defect family and its physical cause.
quiz.solution: Solution. The first is gas porosity (pinholes) — round, smooth pores are the signature of gas bubbles that grew in the liquid before being trapped, caused by a solubility drop at freezing (Sievert's law). The second is microporosity — angular, interdendritic voids caused by the last liquid being cut off from feed metal in the mushy zone, a volume-deficit (shrinkage) cause, not a gas cause.

quiz.q:
Applying Sievert’s law

Problem. At a reference pressure \(p_0\), a melt dissolves \(S_0=25\) ppm of hydrogen. If the effective hydrogen partial pressure is raised to \(4p_0\) (from humid tooling), find the new dissolved concentration.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( [\text{H}]=S_0\sqrt{p/p_0}=25\times\sqrt4=25\times2=50\) ppm — doubling, not quadrupling, because the relationship is a square root of pressure, direct from the \(\text{H}_2\rightleftharpoons2[\text{H}]\) stoichiometry.

quiz.q:
Misrun or cold shut?

Problem. A thin fin on a casting is simply absent — no metal reached that section at all. A separate casting shows a fused-looking seam where two streams met, but the seam pulls apart easily under light stress, with an oxide film visible inside. Identify each defect and state one distinct corrective action for each.
quiz.solution: Solution. The absent fin is a misrun — the stream froze before reaching that section; corrective actions include more superheat or faster filling. The weak, oxide-lined seam is a cold shut — two streams met too cold to truly fuse; correction additionally requires gating that avoids opposed, long-path streams meeting head-on, not superheat alone.

quiz.q: The patternmaker's shrinkage allowance compensates for:
choices: Solid contraction, after the casting is fully frozen
quiz.solution: Only solid-state contraction is left for the pattern oversize to correct.

quiz.q: Low sand permeability combined with high moisture is a classic setup for:
quiz.solution: Steam that cannot vent through the sand is forced into the metal instead.

quiz.q: Foundry fluidity is:
choices: A measured quantity — the distance a test channel fills before freezing shut
quiz.solution: Fluidity is an empirical race between flow and mold-controlled freezing, not a viscosity value.

quiz.q: A misrun differs from a cold shut in that:
choices: A misrun is a section the metal never reached; a cold shut is two streams that met too cold to fuse
They are the same defect with two names
A misrun only occurs in permanent molds
A cold shut is always a gas defect
quiz.solution: Different causes mean different fixes, even though both stem from lost fluidity.

quiz.q: Sievert's law, [H] = S₀√(p/p₀), has a square root because:
choices: Hydrogen gas dissolves by splitting into two atoms, H₂ ⇌ 2[H]
It is an empirical curve fit with no physical basis
Pressure always relates to concentration by a square root
It only applies at very high temperature
quiz.solution: The exponent comes directly from the reaction's stoichiometry.

quiz.q: Pinhole porosity forms because:
choices: Gas solubility drops sharply as the metal solidifies, rejecting dissolved gas
The mold sand is too permeable
quiz.solution: The liquid-to-solid solubility cliff is the entire gas-porosity mechanism.

quiz.q: Round, smooth-walled pores versus jagged, angular voids distinguish:
quiz.solution: Morphology is the diagnostic signature separating these two defect families.

quiz.q: The correct working method for diagnosing a casting defect is:
choices: Read the morphology, trace it to its physical cause, then let the cause pick the fix
Always assume gas porosity by default
Apply the same fix regardless of appearance
quiz.solution: Appearance alone can mislead — cause, not appearance, should select the corrective action.

3.kuwait:
A foundry's molding-sand test sheet — green compressive strength, permeability, moisture, logged shift by shift — predicts that shop's defect profile before a single casting is sectioned: low permeability paired with high moisture reads as a blowhole risk; low strength reads as a penetration-and-wash risk; over-rammed strong sand with hot metal reads as a hot-tear risk. A metallurgist reading that sheet is running §1 in reverse, from cause toward prediction rather than from defect toward diagnosis.

Sectioning a rejected casting and reading its pore morphology under a loupe — round and smooth versus jagged and angular — is standard first-pass failure triage in any melt shop, cheaper and faster than radiography for an initial call on whether a batch problem is gas-related (atmosphere, moisture, melt practice) or feeding-related (riser or gating design), before deciding which department's process actually needs adjusting.

4.lecture:
§1 · The machine, traced

A continuous caster runs Lesson 2's solidification physics at steady state, around the clock. Steel flows ladle to tundish — a steady-head reservoir, doing the pouring basin's job at enormous scale — then into a water-cooled, oscillating copper mold, where a thin solid shell freezes against the walls while the core stays liquid. The strand then travels through spray-cooled support rollers, curving toward horizontal, before a straightener and finally a torch cutoff — which cannot operate until the core is actually solid.

§2 · Shell growth and metallurgical length

The same \(1/\sqrt{t}\) heat-flow physics behind Chvorinov's rule gives the shell-growth law used across continuous casting: \[ \delta = K\sqrt{t}, \] with \(\delta\) the solid shell thickness, \(t\) the time since the strand entered the mold, and \(K\) a solidification constant (typically \(25\)–\(30\ \text{mm/min}^{1/2}\) for water-cooled casters). The strand is fully solid once \(\delta\) reaches the billet's half-thickness \(d/2\); at casting speed \(v\), that happens a fixed distance below the mold — the metallurgical length: \[ L_m = v\,t_{full} = v\left(\frac{d}{2K}\right)^{2}. \] Note that \(t_{full}\) itself depends only on \(d\) and \(K\) — it does not depend on casting speed at all; only the distance \(L_m\) does, since a faster strand simply covers more ground before the same fixed time elapses.

§3 · Reading the numbers

For a \(130\ \text{mm}\) square billet with \(K=27\ \text{mm/min}^{1/2}\) and casting speed \(v=2.6\ \text{m/min}\): \( t_{full}=(65/27)^2\approx5.8\ \text{min} \), so \( L_m=2.6\times5.8\approx15\ \text{m} \) — fifteen meters of strand, through the mold and down the curve, still carries a liquid core; the torch is never positioned before that point. At the mold exit (a \(0.8\ \text{m}\) mold, \(t=0.8/2.6\approx0.31\ \text{min}\)): \( \delta=27\sqrt{0.31}\approx15\ \text{mm} \) — the entire ferrostatic head of that fifteen-meter liquid core is carried by a fifteen-millimeter skin. Push casting speed to \(3.4\ \text{m/min}\) and the exit shell thins to about \(13\ \text{mm}\) while \(L_m\) grows to nearly \(20\ \text{m}\) — casting speed is never a free productivity lever; it trades directly against shell thickness and the depth of the liquid core.

§4 · Quality connection and working method

Billet defects trace to specific subsystems: pinholes to dissolved gas in the melt; internal and centerline cracks to thermal stress and bulging between misaligned or worn support rollers; rhomboidity to uneven mold cooling; centerline segregation to the last, solute-rich liquid at the core — Lesson 3's dendritic argument, running at machine scale. A shell rupture — a breakout — pours liquid steel over the machine and is among the most feared events in a melt shop. Working method: (1) find \(t_{full}\) and \(L_m\) for the section and machine in question; (2) treat any casting-speed change as a three-way trade against shell thickness, breakout risk, and segregation quality, never as a pure throughput knob; (3) trace a specific billet defect back to the subsystem — mold, spray, rollers — most likely responsible.

Key result — metallurgical length

\[ L_m = v\left(\frac{d}{2K}\right)^{2} \]

t_full does not depend on casting speed — only how far the strand travels before reaching it does.

quiz.q:
Metallurgical length

Problem. A 130 mm square billet caster runs \(K=27\ \text{mm/min}^{1/2}\) at \(v=2.6\ \text{m/min}\). Find the total solidification time and the metallurgical length.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( t_{full}=(65/27)^2\approx5.8\ \text{min} \). \( L_m=2.6\times5.8\approx15\ \text{m} \).

quiz.q:
Shell thickness at mold exit

Problem. For the same caster (K = 27 mm/min^0.5, v = 2.6 m/min), the mold is 0.8 m long. Find the shell thickness at mold exit.
quiz.solution: Solution. Time to mold exit: \( t=0.8/2.6\approx0.31\ \text{min} \). \( \delta=27\sqrt{0.31}\approx15\ \text{mm} \).

quiz.q:
The speed trade-off

Problem. If casting speed on the same machine is raised to 3.4 m/min, find the new exit shell thickness and the new metallurgical length. Does t_full change?
quiz.solution: Solution. \( t_{full} \) stays \(\approx5.8\ \text{min}\) — unchanged, since it depends only on d and K. Exit shell: \( t=0.8/3.4\approx0.235\ \text{min} \), \( \delta=27\sqrt{0.235}\approx13.1\ \text{mm} \) — thinner. Metallurgical length: \( L_m=3.4\times5.8\approx19.7\ \text{m} \) — longer. Speed trades a thinner exit shell and a deeper liquid core for more tonnage.

quiz.q: At mold exit, the strand is:
choices: Still mostly liquid inside a thin solid shell
Fully liquid, with no shell at all
quiz.solution: A thin skin, roughly 15 mm here, carries a still-liquid core.

quiz.q: Shell thickness grows:
choices: With the square root of time
quiz.solution: δ = K√t — the same functional form as Chvorinov's rule.

quiz.q: Metallurgical length is:
choices: The fixed distance below the mold where the core finally solidifies
The physical length of the mold itself
Always exactly equal to the mold length
quiz.solution: It marks where the torch may safely operate.

quiz.q: Raising casting speed:
choices: Is not free tonnage — it thins the exit shell and raises breakout risk
Has no effect on shell thickness
Only affects the tundish, not the mold
quiz.solution: Speed trades directly against shell thickness and liquid-core depth.

quiz.q: A breakout is:
choices: A shell rupture releasing liquid steel over the machine
A type of surface defect only
A synonym for the torch cutoff
quiz.solution: One of the most feared routine failure events in a melt shop.

quiz.q: Centerline segregation traces to:
choices: The last, solute-rich liquid at the strand core
quiz.solution: The same dendritic-rejection argument from Lesson 3, at machine scale.

quiz.q: The torch cutoff is positioned:
choices: Only past the metallurgical length, where the core is finally solid
At a fixed distance regardless of the steel grade
quiz.solution: Cutting before the core solidifies would release liquid steel.

quiz.q: Total solidification time, t_full, for a given billet size:
choices: Is independent of casting speed, depending only on d and K
quiz.solution: Only the distance L_m, not the time itself, depends on speed.

4.kuwait:
A regional steel mill's route — melting, ladle refining, billet casting, rolling — makes this lesson one of the most directly transferable process-knowledge units in the whole curriculum. All four reliability-adjacent roles this program targets touch the caster in a different way: mold and oscillator health (reliability), strand containment against breakout (asset integrity), the speed-versus-quality trade-off (production), and mold or segment-roller change planning (maintenance planning) — one machine, four job descriptions.

A billet certificate flagged for centerline segregation, read back against §4, points an investigating engineer toward casting-speed history and core-cooling records before anything else — the defect is generated at the machine, not downstream, and no amount of rolling or heat treatment afterward removes solute banding that formed while the core was still liquid.

5.lecture:
§1 · The expendable-vs-permanent axis, revisited

Lesson 2 opened with the basic split: expendable-mold processes destroy the mold to retrieve the part, permanent-mold processes reuse a metal mold many times. This lesson surveys specific processes along that axis and compares them on the dimensions that actually drive a selection decision — tolerance, surface finish, minimum section thickness, and economics — dimensions the underlying physics from Lessons 2–3 does not address directly.

§2 · Shell molding and investment casting — expendable, refined

Shell molding cures a thin, resin-bonded sand shell against a heated metal pattern — a real step up in dimensional accuracy and surface finish over green sand, still single-use, at moderate cost, well suited to medium-volume precision parts. Investment casting (lost-wax) builds a ceramic shell around a wax pattern, then melts the wax out before pouring metal into the resulting cavity. It achieves the best dimensional accuracy and surface finish of any casting process and can produce very complex internal geometry, but is slow and comparatively expensive per part — reserved for high-value, complex, precision components (turbine blades are the classic case) where machining the same geometry from solid would be impossible or far costlier.

§3 · Permanent-mold and die casting

Gravity permanent-mold casting pours metal by gravity into a reusable metal mold — better surface finish and finer grain than sand casting, from faster metal-mold cooling, but geometry is more limited since the mold must survive many cycles rather than being sacrificed. Die casting injects molten metal — almost always a low-melting-point alloy, aluminum, zinc, or magnesium — under high pressure into a hardened steel die, filling extremely fast. It produces the thinnest sections, the finest surface finish, and the tightest tolerances of any casting process at very high production rates, but tooling cost is the highest of any casting process, demanding real volume to pay for itself, and the fast, turbulent fill entrains air the way Lesson 2's gating discussion warned against, at a far more extreme scale — the resulting porosity generally rules out heat-treating or welding a die casting afterward, since trapped gas expands and blisters the part on reheating.

§4 · Choosing among them — the working method

Given a part: (1) check production volume — low volume favors expendable processes (sand, investment); high volume justifies permanent-mold or die-casting tooling; (2) check geometric complexity and required section thickness — investment casting and die casting both handle thin, complex geometry that sand casting cannot; (3) check surface finish and tolerance — die casting and investment casting both deliver near-net-shape parts needing little or no machining, while sand casting typically needs machining allowance on critical surfaces; (4) check whether the part will be heat-treated or welded afterward — a real constraint against die casting specifically, rarely against the others.

Key checklist — process selection

Volume → tooling cost justified? Complexity/thickness → which processes can hold it? Finish/tolerance → how much machining is budgeted? Post-processing → will it be heat-treated or welded?

quiz.q:
Selecting for high volume, thin walls

Problem. A part needs 500,000 units per year, walls under 1 mm in places, and a smooth as-cast surface with no machining budget. Which casting process fits, and why is sand casting ruled out?
quiz.solution: Solution. Die casting fits: the volume justifies the high tooling cost, and only die casting reliably holds sub-1 mm sections with a smooth as-cast finish. Sand casting is ruled out because it cannot hold sections that thin, and would need machining to reach the required finish and tolerance, which the budget does not allow.

quiz.q:
Selecting for a low-volume complex prototype

Problem. A single prototype turbine blade with complex internal cooling passages is needed, in a nickel superalloy, with excellent surface finish. Which process fits, and why is die casting not an option?
quiz.solution: Solution. Investment casting fits: it achieves the complex internal geometry and fine finish needed, and stays economical even at single-unit volume since a wax pattern costs far less than a hardened steel die. Die casting is not an option because nickel superalloys melt far above what die-casting tooling can survive, and tooling a hardened die for a single prototype would be absurdly uneconomical regardless.

quiz.q:
Diagnosing a blistered die casting

Problem. A die-cast aluminum bracket develops small blisters after being painted and baked at 180°C. Explain the likely cause, tracing back to the casting process itself.
quiz.solution: Solution. High-pressure, high-speed die-cast filling entrains air and gas into the melt, leaving trapped porosity throughout the part. Baking at 180°C after painting heats that trapped gas, which expands and pushes a thin surface layer outward as a blister — the classic reason die castings are generally not subjected to processes involving significant reheating.

quiz.q: Investment casting achieves:
choices: The best dimensional accuracy and surface finish of the processes surveyed
The lowest tooling cost of any process
The highest production rate of any process
quiz.solution: Its precision is the reason it is chosen despite being slow and costly per part.

quiz.q: Die casting is generally limited to:
choices: Low-melting-point alloys, because of die material limits
quiz.solution: Aluminum, zinc, and magnesium are the common die-casting alloys.

quiz.q: Shell molding differs from green sand casting mainly by:
choices: Using a thin, resin-bonded sand shell for better accuracy and finish
quiz.solution: Still expendable, but a real accuracy and finish upgrade over green sand.

quiz.q: The main economic driver for choosing die casting or permanent-mold casting is:
choices: High production volume, to justify the tooling cost
quiz.solution: Expensive, durable tooling only pays for itself at real volume.

quiz.q: Die castings generally cannot be heat-treated afterward because:
choices: Trapped gas porosity expands and blisters the part on reheating
Die-cast alloys never respond to heat treatment
Heat treatment is too expensive to justify
Die castings are always too large for a furnace
quiz.solution: Entrained gas from the fast, turbulent fill is the root cause.

quiz.q: Investment casting is well suited to complex, low-volume, high-value parts because:
choices: Tooling cost is comparatively low even though per-part processing is slow
It is the cheapest process per part at any volume
It requires no pattern of any kind
It only works for very simple shapes
quiz.solution: A wax pattern is far cheaper than hardened die tooling, which offsets slow per-part processing.

quiz.q: Sand casting compared to die casting typically requires:
choices: Thicker minimum sections and more machining allowance
quiz.solution: Gravity filling and a fragile mold both push toward more generous sections.

quiz.q: Gravity permanent-mold casting compared to sand casting generally gives:
choices: Better surface finish and finer grain, from faster metal-mold cooling
quiz.solution: A metal mold extracts heat faster than sand, refining the as-cast grain structure.

5.kuwait:
A procurement decision between a sand-cast and an investment-cast replacement part, for a pump housing needed in low quantity, usually comes down to §4's checklist run in a few minutes: sand casting is cheaper and faster to tool for a single or small-batch order, and the extra machining it needs is a one-time cost worth paying rather than tooling an expensive die for a part that will never be ordered at real volume.

A regional die-casting shop supplying aluminum brackets to an assembly line lives inside §3's porosity constraint daily — any customer request to weld a repair onto a die-cast part, or to heat-treat it for extra strength, gets the same answer: the trapped gas from the process itself makes both operations unreliable, and the honest recommendation is usually a different casting process or a design change instead.

6.lecture:
§1 · Flow stress and the flow curve

Beyond yield, a metal's resistance to further plastic deformation — its flow stress \(Y_f\) — keeps rising with strain through strain hardening, commonly modeled as \[ Y_f = K\varepsilon^{n}, \] with \(K\) the strength coefficient and \(n\) the strain-hardening exponent, both material constants read from a tension test. The flow curve — true stress against true strain — is what a forming process actually pushes against, not the simpler engineering stress-strain curve, which is only valid up to necking. Forming operations generally impose a stress state that suppresses necking, so the flow curve remains meaningful at strains far beyond where an ordinary tension specimen would already have failed.

§2 · Hot, warm, and cold working — defined by homologous temperature

The dividing line between these regimes is not an absolute temperature but homologous temperature, \(T/T_m\), both measured on an absolute (Kelvin) scale. Cold working, below roughly \(0.3\,T_m\), lets strain hardening accumulate fully — good surface finish and precision, but rising force and a limited total deformation before an anneal is needed. Hot working, above roughly \(0.6\,T_m\), runs hot enough that dynamic recrystallization continuously undoes strain hardening as it forms, so flow stress stays roughly constant regardless of strain — enormous shape change becomes possible for modest force, at the cost of poorer surface finish, looser tolerance, and oxide scale from the high temperature. Warm working sits between the two, trading some of each advantage.

§3 · Strain-rate sensitivity

Flow stress also depends on how fast the material is deformed, particularly at hot-working temperature: \( Y_f=C\dot\varepsilon^{m} \), with \(m\) the strain-rate sensitivity exponent. At cold-working temperature, \(m\) is nearly zero — rate barely matters. At hot-working temperature, \(m\) rises meaningfully, and a higher \(m\) genuinely helps resist necking: a region that starts thinning locally strains faster there, and a high-\(m\) material responds by getting locally stronger, self-correcting the flaw rather than running away toward failure — the basis of superplastic forming in carefully controlled, very-high-\(m\) alloys.

§4 · Friction, lubrication, and workability

Friction at the tool-workpiece interface resists metal flow and raises force — and, as later lessons on rolling and forging show, is sometimes the very mechanism a process depends on, sometimes purely a cost to minimize. Lubrication regimes differ sharply by temperature: hot working often runs with no lubricant, or a high-temperature glass or graphite coating; cold working uses oils, soaps, or coatings chosen to survive without breaking down under pressure. Workability — how much deformation a material tolerates before cracking — depends on both the material's inherent ductility and the stress state a specific process imposes: compressive-dominated processes (forging, rolling) are far more forgiving than tension-dominated ones (wire drawing), which is precisely why drawing carries a hard theoretical strain ceiling that compression-based processes do not share in the same way.

Key thresholds — homologous temperature

Cold work: \(T<0.3\,T_m\). Hot work: \(T>0.6\,T_m\) (both absolute). \( Y_f=K\varepsilon^n \) (cold, strain-hardening) vs. roughly flat (hot, dynamic recrystallization).

What this lesson assumes

  • Recrystallization and grain structure (Materials Science II) — the mechanism behind hot working's flat curve.

quiz.q:
Flow stress at a given strain

Problem. A steel has \(K=500\ \text{MPa}\), \(n=0.25\). Find its flow stress at a true strain \(\varepsilon=0.40\), and explain what that number means physically.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( Y_f=500\times0.40^{0.25}\approx398\ \text{MPa} \). This is the stress the material resists further plastic flow with at that accumulated strain — well above its original yield strength, since strain hardening has already raised its resistance substantially.

quiz.q:
Locating the working regimes

Problem. A metal melts at 1000°C (1273 K). Find the approximate temperatures, in °C, where hot working begins (T > 0.6 Tm) and where cold working ends (T < 0.3 Tm).
quiz.solution: Solution. Hot-working threshold: \(0.6\times1273\approx764\ \text{K}\approx491^\circ\text{C}\). Cold-working threshold: \(0.3\times1273\approx382\ \text{K}\approx109^\circ\text{C}\). Below about 109°C is cold work, above about 491°C is hot work, and the range between is warm work for this material.

quiz.q:
Compression vs. tension in workability

Problem. Explain why forging can push a metal to very high strains without failure, while wire drawing has a hard theoretical strain ceiling for a single pass, even for the identical material.
quiz.solution: Solution. Forging imposes a mostly compressive stress state, which suppresses necking and void growth and tolerates very large strains. Wire drawing imposes tension at the die exit; because required drawing stress rises with strain just as the material's own flow stress does, there is a strain beyond which drawing stress would exceed the wire's flow stress and it would simply neck and fail at the exit — a limit compression-dominated processes do not share.

quiz.q: Flow stress typically follows:
choices: Yf = K·ε^n, rising with true strain
Yf = constant, regardless of strain, in all cases
quiz.solution: Strain hardening raises flow stress with accumulated strain — at least in cold working.

quiz.q: The flow curve differs from a basic engineering stress-strain curve because:
choices: It remains valid to much higher strains, since forming stress states generally suppress necking
It is only valid up to yield
It is identical to the engineering curve
It only applies to hot working
quiz.solution: An ordinary tension curve fails past necking; forming keeps going well beyond that point.

quiz.q: Cold working is defined as occurring:
choices: Below about 0.3 times the absolute melting temperature
Above about 0.6 times the absolute melting temperature
quiz.solution: The threshold is homologous (relative to melting point), not an absolute number.

quiz.q: Hot working is defined as occurring:
choices: Above about 0.6 times the absolute melting temperature
Below about 0.3 times the absolute melting temperature
quiz.solution: Homologous temperature, not an absolute value, sets the threshold for every material.

quiz.q: In hot working, flow stress stays roughly constant with strain because:
choices: Dynamic recrystallization continuously undoes strain hardening as it forms
quiz.solution: Recrystallization resets the hardened structure faster than it can accumulate.

quiz.q: Strain-rate sensitivity (m) is most significant at:
choices: It is identical at all temperatures
quiz.solution: m is near zero in cold work but rises meaningfully in hot work.

quiz.q: A compression-dominated process like forging tolerates much higher strain than wire drawing mainly because:
choices: Compression suppresses necking and void growth, while drawing's tensile state does not
Drawing always runs hotter than forging
quiz.solution: The stress state, not the material, is the key difference.

quiz.q: Workability depends on:
choices: Both the material's inherent ductility and the stress state the specific process imposes
The material's ductility alone, regardless of process
The process alone, regardless of material
quiz.solution: The same material can be far more or less workable depending on how it is being deformed.

6.kuwait:
A shop deciding whether to hot-forge or cold-form a bracket is running §2's trade-off directly: hot working buys a large shape change in one hit at the cost of scale and looser tolerance, needing a machining pass afterward on critical surfaces; cold working buys precision and surface finish directly but limits how much shape change is possible before an intermediate anneal is required — the same decision this course's forging and rolling lessons apply to specific processes.

A wire or rod producer scheduling anneals between drawing passes is managing §1's flow curve in real time: skipping a scheduled anneal to save a cycle pushes the material further up its own hardening curve than the next pass was designed for, raising the odds of a wire break at the die rather than a clean draw — a maintenance-adjacent quality decision as much as a metallurgical one.

7.lecture:
§1 · The bite condition — checked before anything else

Flat rolling squeezes a slab or bar between two driven rolls, reducing thickness from \(h_0\) to \(h_f\). The draft, \(d=h_0-h_f\), cannot exceed \[ d_{max}=\mu^2R, \] or the rolls simply skid instead of biting into the stock — friction is what drives the material through the pass, not merely a cost to minimize as in some other forming processes. Checking this limit is the first step of any rolling calculation, before force, torque, or power are worth computing at all.

§2 · Contact length, force, torque, and power

Once a pass is confirmed possible, the projected contact length is \( L=\sqrt{R(h_0-h_f)} \), and the remaining estimates follow directly: \[ F=\bar Y_f\,w\,L,\qquad T=0.5\,F\,L,\qquad P=2\pi N F L, \] with \(\bar Y_f\) the mean flow stress over the pass, \(w\) the strip width, and \(N\) the roll speed. For a hot-rolling pass — \(h_0=30\ \text{mm}\to h_f=22\ \text{mm}\), \(R=300\ \text{mm}\), \(w=250\ \text{mm}\), \(\mu=0.30\), \(\bar Y_f\approx180\ \text{MPa}\), \(N=50\ \text{rpm}\): the draft \(d=8\ \text{mm}\) is comfortably inside \(d_{max}=0.09\times300=27\ \text{mm}\); \(L=\sqrt{300\times8}\approx49\ \text{mm}\); \(F\approx180\times0.250\times0.049\approx2.2\ \text{MN}\) — roughly 225 tonnes-force on the stand; torque per roll \(\approx54\ \text{kN·m}\); power \(\approx566\ \text{kW}\).

§3 · Hot vs. cold rolling — different products, not different settings

Hot rolling runs rough and grippy on purpose — friction is welcomed, because it buys large shape change cheaply above the recrystallization temperature (Lesson 6's hot-working regime), where flow stress stays comparatively low and roughly flat with strain. Cold rolling lubricates deliberately and accepts smaller drafts per pass, because it is selling a different outcome entirely: strain hardening raises strength, and the surface arrives clean, precise, and close to final dimension. These are two different products from two different working regimes, not the same process run at two settings.

§4 · Reading the defect map

Wavy edges and center buckle trace to roll bending, making thickness — and therefore elongation — uneven across the strip width; crown and camber management on the rolls is the fix. Alligatoring, a centerline split at the exit, usually traces further upstream to casting quality (Lesson 4's centerline segregation returning at the mill). Rolled-in scale and seams trace to descaling practice and the incoming billet's own surface condition. In a rebar mill specifically, rib and grade-mark quality add roll wear as a first-class variable worth tracking, not an afterthought.

Key limit — the bite condition

\[ d_{max} = \mu^2 R \]

No friction, no bite, no process — but the same friction that drives the pass also raises force and wear.

Checking the bite condition

Problem. A rolling pass has \(\mu=0.30\), \(R=300\ \text{mm}\), and draft \(d=8\ \text{mm}\). Is the pass possible, and by how much margin?
quiz.solution: Solution. \( d_{max}=\mu^2R=0.09\times300=27\ \text{mm} \). Since \(8\ \text{mm}<27\ \text{mm}\), the pass is possible, with substantial margin before the rolls would skid.

quiz.q:
Roll force, torque, and power

Problem. For the same pass (\(R=300\ \text{mm}\), \(d=8\ \text{mm}\), \(w=250\ \text{mm}\), \(\bar Y_f=180\ \text{MPa}\), \(N=50\ \text{rpm}\)), find the contact length, roll force, torque per roll, and power.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( L=\sqrt{300\times8}\approx49\ \text{mm} \). \( F=180\times10^6\times0.250\times0.049\approx2.2\ \text{MN} \). \( T=0.5\times2.2\times10^6\times0.049\approx54\ \text{kN·m} \). \( P=2\pi\times(50/60)\times2.2\times10^6\times0.049\approx566\ \text{kW} \).

quiz.q:
Why alligatoring points upstream

Problem. A rolled bar splits along its centerline at the mill exit. Explain why this defect is usually traced back to the casting stage rather than to the rolling pass itself.
quiz.solution: Solution. Alligatoring at the centerline typically reflects a pre-existing weakness from centerline segregation in the cast billet (Lesson 4) — solute-rich, weaker material at the core — that rolling then exposes and splits open rather than causes. Fixing the rolling schedule alone would not address a defect whose root cause is upstream in the casting process.

quiz.q: The friction bite condition states that draft cannot exceed:
quiz.solution: Beyond this, the rolls skid rather than bite.

quiz.q: In rolling, friction is best described as:
choices: The mechanism that drives the pass, not merely a cost
Purely a cost to be minimized, exactly as in most other processes
quiz.solution: Without friction there is no bite at all — rolling depends on it directly.

quiz.q: Contact length in flat rolling is given by:
quiz.solution: The projected contact length feeds directly into force, torque, and power.

quiz.q: Hot rolling runs rough and grippy on purpose because:
choices: It buys large shape change cheaply above the recrystallization temperature
It produces the best surface finish
It requires no lubrication study at all
quiz.solution: Grip matters more than finish when the goal is large, cheap shape change.

quiz.q: Cold rolling differs from hot rolling mainly by:
choices: Selling strain-hardened strength and a clean, precise surface
quiz.solution: Cold rolling is a different product, not merely a tighter-tolerance version of hot rolling.

quiz.q: Wavy edges and center buckle in a rolled strip trace to:
choices: Roll bending causing uneven thickness across the width
quiz.solution: Crown and camber management on the rolls addresses this specifically.

quiz.q: Rolled-in scale and seams typically trace to:
choices: Descaling practice and the incoming billet's surface condition
quiz.solution: Surface defects usually originate upstream, at the billet surface or descaling stage.

quiz.q: Roll force is given by:
quiz.solution: Mean flow stress times contact width times contact length.

7.kuwait:
A rolling mill's pass schedule is built around the bite condition from §1 before anything else — each stand's maximum draft is checked against μ²R for that pass, and a schedule that ignores this limit simply will not run, regardless of how the force and power numbers downstream might otherwise look on paper.

Stand force estimates from §2 size more than the rolls themselves: the same numbers set bearing and gearbox loads, which is exactly why a rolling mill's reliability program tracks these same forces as condition-monitoring inputs, not just design-stage calculations — a stand drawing noticeably more force than its schedule predicts, at the same draft and speed, is usually flagging a roll or bearing problem before it becomes a stoppage.

8.lecture:
§1 · Open-die, impression-die, and flashless forging

Open-die forging uses simple, largely flat or lightly contoured dies that do not enclose the workpiece — metal flows outward freely as it compresses, good for large, simple shapes and preliminary shaping, with limited dimensional control. Impression-die (closed-die) forging constrains flow into a shaped cavity, giving far better dimensional control and complex shapes — but the excess metal has to go somewhere, which is what flash is for. Flashless forging matches starting billet volume precisely to the cavity so no flash forms at all, at the cost of needing tightly controlled billet volume and greater die and press investment.

§2 · Upsetting analysis — force including friction's barreling penalty

For a cylinder upset from \(h_0\) to \(h\), true strain is \( \varepsilon=\ln(h_0/h) \) and flow stress follows the familiar \( Y_f=K\varepsilon^{n} \). Friction at the die faces resists the metal's outward radial flow near the platens more than at mid-height, producing the classic bulge — barreling. This friction penalty is captured by a multiplier \[ K_f = 1+\frac{0.4\mu D}{h}, \] which grows as the part gets shorter and wider (\(D/h\) increases) — the force penalty from friction worsens as upsetting proceeds, it does not stay constant. With the current cross-section \(A=V/h\) from volume conservation, force is \( F=K_f\,Y_f\,A \).

§3 · Reading the numbers

Cold-upsetting a low-carbon steel slug, \(d_0=50\ \text{mm}\), \(h_0=70\ \text{mm}\to h=40\ \text{mm}\), \(K=500\ \text{MPa}\), \(n=0.25\), \(\mu=0.25\): volume \(V=137{,}400\ \text{mm}^3\), final area \(A=V/h=3{,}436\ \text{mm}^2\), diameter \(D=66.1\ \text{mm}\), strain \(\varepsilon=\ln(70/40)\approx0.56\), \( Y_f=500\times0.56^{0.25}\approx432\ \text{MPa} \), \( K_f=1+0.4\times0.25\times66.1/40\approx1.17 \), giving \( F\approx1.17\times432\times3{,}436\times10^{-3}\approx1.73\ \text{MN} \) — roughly 176 tonnes-force, and this is the peak force, occurring at the end of the stroke where both \(Y_f\) and \(K_f\) are largest.

§4 · Grain flow and flash — what they genuinely buy

Forging refines structure, closes internal porosity, and — critically — aligns inclusion stringers and grain flow along the part's contour, rather than cutting across it the way machining from bar stock would. Properties improve specifically along the direction grain flow follows, particularly fatigue resistance; the advantage shrinks, and can even reverse, across the flow — so "forged is always stronger than cast or machined" is an oversimplification that ignores loading direction. Flash in impression-die forging is not wasted metal by accident: the thin flash land deliberately chokes outward flow, building the internal cavity pressure that forces metal into the last, hardest-to-fill corners of the die — engineered waste, trimmed off afterward, not an oversight.

Key multiplier — friction in upsetting

\[ K_f = 1+\frac{0.4\mu D}{h} \]

Grows as the part shortens and widens — peak force lands at the end of the stroke, not the start.

quiz.q:
Why a machined hook is not equivalent

Problem. A forged crane hook is loaded primarily by tension pulling along its curved shape. Explain why a machined-from-bar replacement, cut to the identical outer geometry, is not automatically equivalent in fatigue strength.
quiz.solution: Solution. The forged hook's grain flow follows the curved contour, aligned with the primary load path, giving superior fatigue resistance along that path. A hook machined from straight bar cuts across that natural flow wherever the part's curve departs from the bar's original grain direction, leaving grain boundaries and inclusions crossing the load path — potential fatigue-crack initiation sites the forged part's aligned flow avoids.

quiz.q:
Fixing an underfilled die corner

Problem. An impression-die forging comes out with an incompletely filled corner. Is increasing the starting billet volume or decreasing the flash-land thickness more likely to fix the underfill, and why?
quiz.solution: Solution. Decreasing the flash-land thickness is more likely to help: a thinner land chokes outward flow more strongly, building higher internal cavity pressure that forces metal into the unfilled corner. Simply adding billet volume without changing the flash geometry mostly produces more flash — more waste — not necessarily higher fill pressure at the specific corner that is underfilled.

quiz.q: Peak upsetting force typically occurs:

quiz.q: Forged parts show improved fatigue resistance specifically:

quiz.solution: Direction matters — the advantage is not uniform in all loading directions.

quiz.q: Flash in impression-die forging exists to:
choices: Build internal pressure that forces metal into the last, hardest-to-fill cavity corners

quiz.solution: Choking outward flow is what builds the fill-completing pressure.

quiz.q: Barreling in upsetting occurs because:

9.lecture:
§1 · Two die processes, opposite stress states

Extrusion pushes metal through a die under compression — a ram compresses the billet, forcing it through a reducing opening. That compressive state tolerates enormous single-pass strains (extrusion ratios of 40 or more for aluminum) and can even improve workability, since compression tends to close internal voids rather than open them. Wire and rod drawing pulls metal through a die instead — the already-reduced material downstream of the die supplies the pulling force, which means the drawn material itself must survive that tensile stress without yielding. That one difference in sign — compression versus tension — explains both processes' products: massive sections from extrusion, kilometres of fine wire from drawing.

§2 · Extrusion: ratio, empirical strain, pressure

Extrusion ratio is \( r_x=A_0/A_f \). Johnson's empirical extrusion strain folds ideal work, friction, and redundant shear into one number, \( \varepsilon_x=a+b\ln r_x \) (typically \(a\approx0.8\), \(b\approx1.2\)–\(1.5\)), giving a ram pressure estimate \( p=\bar Y_f\,\varepsilon_x \).

§3 · Drawing: stress, and the survival check

Drawing stress is \[ \sigma_d=\bar Y_f\left(1+\frac{\mu}{\alpha}\right)\phi\ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right), \] with \(\mu\) the die friction, \(\alpha\) the die half-angle in radians, and \(\phi\geq1\) a redundant-work factor. The absolute constraint: \(\sigma_d\) must stay well below the drawn wire's own flow stress at that exit strain, or the wire yields and necks at the die exit instead of drawing cleanly through. This gives the classical result that even a perfectly frictionless, ideal pass cannot exceed roughly \(63\)% area reduction (\(\varepsilon=1\)); real passes, carrying friction and redundant work, run well below that. For a copper drawing pass, \(3.50\to3.03\ \text{mm}\) (\(25\)% area reduction), \(K=300\ \text{MPa}\), \(n=0.50\), \(\mu=0.07\), \(\alpha=6^\circ\): \(\bar Y_f\approx107\ \text{MPa}\), \(\phi\approx1.05\), giving \( \sigma_d\approx54\ \text{MPa} \) — comfortably below the drawn wire's own flow stress of \(\approx161\ \text{MPa}\), a safe pass with real margin.

§4 · Tandem drawing — the working method

Because a single pass is capped well below the theoretical ceiling by real friction and redundant-work margins, producing fine wire from a much larger rod requires many passes in sequence — tandem drawing. Each pass is re-checked against its own \(\sigma_d\)-versus-flow-stress safety margin; because strain hardening accumulates pass over pass, that margin shrinks as drawing proceeds, which is exactly why intermediate anneals are scheduled partway through a tandem sequence — resetting accumulated hardening so later passes still have a comfortable margin, rather than letting it shrink toward zero and risking a break.

Key constraint — drawing stress

\(\sigma_d\) must stay below the drawn wire's own flow stress — the real, binding limit, well short of the ideal 63% ceiling.

quiz.q:
Checking a drawing pass for safety

Problem. Copper wire is drawn from 3.50 to 3.03 mm (25% area reduction), \(K=300\ \text{MPa}\), \(n=0.50\), \(\mu=0.07\), \(\alpha=6^\circ\). Given \(\bar Y_f\approx107\ \text{MPa}\) and \(\phi\approx1.05\), find the drawing stress and confirm the pass is safe.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( \sigma_d=107\times(1+0.07/0.105)\times1.05\times\ln(1/0.75)\approx54\ \text{MPa} \). The drawn wire’s own flow stress is \(300\times0.288^{0.5}\approx161\ \text{MPa}\), far above 54 MPa — a safe pass with real margin.

quiz.q:
Estimating extrusion pressure

Problem. A 200 mm diameter aluminum billet is extruded to 50 mm diameter. Find the extrusion ratio, and using \(a=0.8\), \(b=1.5\), \(\bar Y_f\approx70\ \text{MPa}\), estimate the ram pressure.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( r_x=(200/50)^2=16 \). \( \varepsilon_x=0.8+1.5\ln16\approx0.8+4.16\approx4.96 \). \( p\approx70\times4.96\approx347\ \text{MPa} \).

quiz.q:
How many passes, and why anneal?

Problem. A wire must go from 10 mm to 3 mm diameter (91% area reduction total), but each pass is limited to about 30% area reduction before the safety margin runs thin. Estimate the number of passes needed, and explain why anneals are scheduled partway through.
quiz.solution: Solution. Each pass leaves 70% of the entering area; reaching \((3/10)^2\approx9\)% of the original area needs \(n\) where \(0.70^n\approx0.09\), giving \(n\approx6.7\), so about 7 passes. Anneals are scheduled partway through because strain hardening accumulates pass over pass, raising the wire's flow stress and shrinking the safety margin between drawing stress and that flow stress — an anneal resets the accumulated hardening so later passes keep a workable margin instead of drawing toward a break.

quiz.q: Extrusion can achieve much higher single-pass strains than drawing mainly because:

quiz.q: The theoretical maximum area reduction for an ideal, frictionless single drawing pass is approximately:

quiz.q: Drawing stress must stay below:

quiz.q: Real drawing passes run well below the theoretical ideal limit mainly because:
choices: Friction and redundant (non-ideal) work add to the required drawing stress
The theoretical limit is simply wrong
Real dies have no friction at all
Real wire has no flow stress
quiz.solution: Both effects raise the actual stress needed above the frictionless ideal.

quiz.q: Tandem drawing machines with intermediate anneals exist because:

quiz.solution: Resetting hardening periodically keeps every pass within a safe margin.

9.kuwait:
A wire-drawing line's die-wear and lubricant-condition log is a direct read on §3's safety-margin logic: a die wearing out of round, or a lubricant breaking down under pressure, raises effective friction and redundant work, which raises drawing stress at the same nominal reduction — narrowing the margin against the wire's own flow stress until a wire break interrupts the line, often before any other symptom shows up on a routine visual inspection.

Extrusion tooling for aluminum sections is chosen and maintained around §2's pressure estimate: a die designed for a modest extrusion ratio pressed into service for a much higher ratio than it was rated for is a predictable, avoidable cause of premature die wear or failure, traceable directly back to the ram-pressure number a design review should have checked before the tooling order was placed.

10.lecture:
§1 · The three-step process

Powder production — most commonly atomization, spraying molten metal into fine droplets that solidify as powder — sets particle size and shape, which strongly affects how well the powder compacts and flows. Compaction presses powder in a rigid die under high pressure into a green compact: a fragile, porous shape held together only by mechanical interlocking and cold welding at particle contact points, typically around \(80\)–\(85\)% of theoretical density — not yet a real structural part. Sintering heats that compact, below its melting point (typically \(70\)–\(90\)% of \(T_m\), Lesson 6's homologous-temperature logic returning here), in a controlled atmosphere, letting atomic diffusion bond particles together, close pores, and build real mechanical strength.

§2 · Density is the controlling quality variable

Nearly every mechanical property of a PM part — strength, ductility, fatigue life, corrosion resistance — scales with its final density relative to the fully-dense wrought material of the same composition. A part at \(90\)% density can fall meaningfully short of wrought strength and ductility, because residual pores act as stress concentrators and crack-initiation sites — the same flaw logic that governs fracture mechanics generally. Density is set primarily by compaction pressure and sintering temperature and time, both pushing the compact closer to full density with diminishing returns; it approaches, but never quite reaches, \(100\)%. Secondary operations — repressing, infiltration with a lower-melting metal, resin impregnation — can push density and properties higher still, at added cost, used only when base sintered properties fall short of the target.

§3 · Where PM wins

PM excels at small, complex parts at high volume: powder goes from raw material to near-net shape in one compaction step, needing little or no machining — the same volume-justifies-tooling economics as die casting. PM also uniquely delivers engineered porosity: a self-lubricating bronze or iron bearing is deliberately made at less than full density, then oil-impregnated into the connected pore network, so the part itself becomes a distributed oil reservoir — a property a fully-dense machined bearing simply cannot provide. Finally, PM handles materials no other route can: tungsten's melting point defeats casting, and a cemented-carbide cutting insert — hard tungsten-carbide particles bonded in a tougher cobalt matrix — is a composite structure that cannot be created by casting or by machining a homogeneous bar at all.

§4 · Working method — selecting or specifying a PM part

(1) Translate the property target into a required density, or deliberately accept a lower density if porosity itself is the desired feature, as in a self-lubricating bearing. (2) Confirm volume justifies compaction die tooling cost, the same volume logic as Lesson 5's casting comparison. (3) Confirm the geometry is genuinely PM-favorable — no deep re-entrant features the die cannot eject — and that the material is either economical or genuinely hard to process any other way. (4) Decide sintered-only versus a secondary densification step based on whether base sintered properties actually meet the target.

Key fact — density controls properties

PM mechanical properties scale with final density relative to fully-dense material. Density is set by compaction pressure and sintering temperature/time — approaching, never reaching, 100%.

quiz.q:
Density and expected strength

Problem. A PM iron part is compacted to 82% density (green), then sintered to 93% density. Would this part be expected to match the tensile strength of a fully-dense wrought iron of the same composition? Explain.
quiz.solution: Solution. No — at 93% density, roughly 7% of the cross-section is porosity, and those pores act as stress concentrators and crack-initiation sites. Strength, and especially ductility, will fall meaningfully short of the fully-dense wrought material, even though sintering has closed much of the original green porosity.

quiz.q:
Why a solid bearing cannot do the same job

Problem. A self-lubricating bronze bearing is specified at 80% density with the remaining pore volume oil-impregnated. Explain why a fully-dense (100%) bronze bearing, machined to the identical external shape, could not deliver the same function.
quiz.solution: Solution. A fully-dense bearing has no connected internal pore network to hold and slowly release oil during operation. Self-lubrication comes specifically from the engineered porosity acting as a distributed oil reservoir — a feature only a deliberately sub-full-density PM part provides; a solid bearing would need external lubrication instead.

quiz.q:
Why PM, not casting or machining

Problem. A cutting-tool insert must combine extreme hardness (tungsten carbide) with enough toughness to survive interrupted cuts (a cobalt binder). Explain why this combination is made by powder metallurgy rather than casting or machining from a wrought bar.
quiz.solution: Solution. Tungsten carbide's melting point is far too high for casting, and the hard carbide phase and tougher cobalt binder cannot be combined by machining a homogeneous wrought bar, since no such wrought two-phase material exists. PM presses and sinters the two powders directly into a hard-particle-in-tough-matrix composite that cannot be created by any other route.

11.lecture:
§1 · Property indices meet fixed vs. variable cost

Fixed cost — tooling, dies, molds, setup — is paid once (or per changeover) regardless of how many parts are made. Variable cost — material, energy, direct labor per cycle — scales directly with quantity. Total cost for a quantity \(Q\) is \[ C(Q) = \text{Fixed} + \text{Variable}\times Q. \] Two processes capable of making the identical part generally carry very different fixed and variable costs — a process with cheap tooling but a high per-part cost (sand casting) against one with expensive tooling but a low per-part cost (die casting) is exactly the volume logic Lessons 5 and 10 already touched, now made explicit and quantitative.

§2 · Break-even volume — the working method

Setting two processes' total-cost lines equal and solving for \(Q\) gives the break-even volume: below it, the low-fixed-cost process is cheaper; above it, the low-variable-cost process is cheaper, by a widening margin as volume grows further. This single number is genuinely decision-driving, not academic — comparing a customer's actual expected volume against it is, in practice, how a manufacturing engineer chooses between competing processes more often than any single technical merit argument alone.

§3 · Reading a real decision

Process A: \(\$2{,}000\) fixed, \(\$8\)/part. Process B: \(\$20{,}000\) fixed, \(\$2\)/part. Break-even: \( Q_{be}=(20{,}000-2{,}000)/(8-2)=3{,}000 \) units. Below \(3{,}000\) units, Process A wins; above it, Process B wins, and the gap widens quickly — at \(10{,}000\) units, Process A costs \(\$2{,}000+\$80{,}000=\$82{,}000\) against Process B's \(\$20{,}000+\$20{,}000=\$40{,}000\), roughly half. The method generalizes past two processes: with three or more candidates, compute every pair's break-even volume, and the cheapest process at any target volume is whichever line sits lowest at that specific \(Q\) — not necessarily the same process across the whole volume range.

§4 · Beyond the break-even number

Break-even analysis assumes cost is the only criterion — a real decision still needs Lesson 5's and Lesson 10's technical screening first: can the process even achieve the required geometry, tolerance, finish, and material at all? Comparing costs between two processes that cannot both actually make the part is a wasted exercise. The analysis also assumes static per-part costs, ignoring that variable cost itself can fall with cumulative volume through learning-curve effects, and that any demand forecast carries real uncertainty. A decision made right at a razor-thin break-even margin is riskier than the arithmetic alone suggests — a prudent engineer builds in margin rather than committing to whichever process wins by a small amount at the forecast volume alone.

Key formula — break-even volume

\[ C(Q)=\text{Fixed}+\text{Variable}\times Q,\qquad Q_{be}=\frac{\text{Fixed}_B-\text{Fixed}_A}{\text{Variable}_A-\text{Variable}_B}. \]

quiz.solution: Real forecasts carry uncertainty the break-even number itself does not capture.

11.kuwait:
A quoting engineer comparing sand casting against die casting for a new bracket order is running §3's exact arithmetic before naming a price: the customer's stated annual volume, checked against the break-even point between the two processes' actual fixed and variable costs, decides which process gets quoted — and a customer whose volume forecast sits close to that break-even point is worth a direct conversation about forecast confidence before committing to expensive die tooling on a volume that might not materialize.

A plant evaluating whether to bring a currently outsourced part in-house is running the identical break-even logic in a different guise: in-house production usually looks like Process B (higher fixed cost for equipment/tooling, lower variable cost per part) against the outsourced supplier's per-part quote acting as Process A (zero fixed cost to the plant, but a higher effective per-part price) — the same Q_be arithmetic, applied to a make-or-buy decision rather than a choice between two manufacturing processes.
